level01.title: Level 01 Prep: Foundational AI Literacy
level01.overview: Establish baseline human-in-the-loop competencies, operator accountability boundaries, and introductory algorithmic transparency standards required under EU AI Act Article 4 deployer obligations.

level02.title: Level 02 Prep: Executive Corporate Governance
level02.target: Procurement managers, legal leads, and C-Suite officers.
level02.overview: Develop executive-grade procurement oversight, vendor disclosure auditing, and corporate liability exposure mitigation aligned with institutional Article 4 governance expectations.

level03.title: Level 03 Prep: High-Risk System Auditing
level03.target: Technical auditors and algorithmic validation architects.
level03.overview: Master high-risk deployment auditing workflows, complex human-in-the-loop validation, data lineage provenance checks, and cryptographic ledger hash orchestration on WaqfLedger.tech.

lesson1.title: Human-in-the-Loop Operator Boundaries
lesson1.intro: Article 4 requires deployers to ensure staff can meaningfully supervise AI-assisted outputs before those outputs influence end-user decisions.
lesson1.paragraph1: Operational personnel must recognize when an algorithmic recommendation crosses from informational support into a decision that requires documented human validation.
lesson1.paragraph2: This module maps the escalation threshold — the point at which an operator must pause automation, invoke override protocols, or route the output to independent review.

step1.label: Detect
step1.body: Monitor AI output signals against pre-defined risk categories.
step1.item1: Flag confidence scores below institutional threshold

step2.label: Evaluate
step2.body: Apply Article 4 escalation criteria before release.
step2.item2: Confirm human reviewer is authorized for override

step3.label: Document
step3.body: Record intervention decision for audit provenance.
step3.item1: Log timestamp, reviewer identity, and rationale
step3.item2: Prepare WaqfLedger.tech credential trail if escalated

lesson2.title: Risk Recognition & Transparency Disclosures
lesson2.intro: Deployers must communicate known limitations of AI systems to downstream users and internal stakeholders with sufficient clarity.
lesson2.paragraph1: Transparency is not a marketing exercise — it is a regulatory obligation that shapes how operators interpret and challenge algorithmic outputs.
lesson2.paragraph2: Learn to distinguish between informational disclosures (system capabilities) and operational warnings (known failure modes requiring human oversight).

step1.label: Inventory
step1.body: Catalog system capabilities and known limitations.
step1.item2: Document model version and training cutoff

step2.label: Communicate
step2.body: Deliver disclosures to operators and end users.
step2.item2: Embed warnings in workflow UI touchpoints

lesson3.title: Baseline Accountability & Incident Response
lesson3.intro: When AI-assisted processes produce adverse outcomes, deployers bear accountability for whether adequate human oversight was exercised.
lesson3.paragraph1: Incident response under Article 4 begins with demonstrating that operators possessed sufficient literacy to recognize, escalate, and intervene appropriately.
lesson3.paragraph2: This module establishes the minimum documentation standard for post-incident review and regulatory inquiry readiness.

step1.label: Contain
step1.body: Isolate the AI-assisted process upon adverse signal detection.

step3.label: Remediate
step3.body: Implement corrective literacy and oversight measures.
step3.item1: Schedule targeted re-training if gap identified
step3.item2: Update escalation protocols and re-certify operators

q1.prompt: An AI chatbot recommends denying a customer refund. What is your first Article 4-compliant action?
q1.context: Consider whether the output requires human validation before affecting the customer's fundamental economic rights.

options.a: Accept the AI recommendation and process the denial immediately
options.b: Pause automation and escalate to an authorized human reviewer before the decision affects the customer
options.c: Request the chatbot generate additional justification text, then proceed
options.d: Forward the case to the vendor support team without internal review

q2.prompt: Your team deploys a new AI scheduling tool without operator briefing materials. Which Article 4 obligation is most immediately at risk?
q2.context: Evaluate transparency and sufficient AI literacy among staff operating the system.

options.a: Cross-border data transfer authorization under GDPR
options.c: Deployer AI literacy and transparency obligations for operating staff

q3.prompt: An operator overrides an AI recommendation but does not log the decision. What compliance gap does this create?
q3.context: Think about audit provenance and demonstrable human oversight under institutional thresholds.

options.a: Non-compliance with model retraining frequency mandates
options.b: Loss of audit provenance and inability to demonstrate human oversight
options.c: Breach of vendor service-level agreement terms

lesson1.title: AI Procurement Due-Diligence Framework
lesson1.intro: Executive leaders must validate that vendor AI systems meet Article 4 literacy and oversight requirements before institutional adoption.
lesson1.paragraph1: Procurement diligence extends beyond technical specifications to include deployer training obligations, transparency documentation, and liability allocation clauses.
lesson1.paragraph2: This module structures the executive checklist for evaluating third-party AI vendors against EU AI Act compliance expectations.

step1.label: Scope
step1.body: Define AI system role and institutional risk exposure.

step2.label: Audit
step2.body: Review vendor transparency and literacy support packages.

step3.label: Contract
step3.body: Embed governance clauses and liability boundaries.
step3.item2: Require incident notification and audit access rights

lesson2.title: Corporate Liability & Risk Attribution Models
lesson2.intro: C-Suite officers must understand how AI literacy gaps translate into corporate liability exposure under the EU AI Act.
lesson2.paragraph1: Risk attribution models map which organizational roles bear responsibility when AI-assisted decisions produce regulatory or reputational harm.
lesson2.paragraph2: Legal leads use these models to structure board reporting, insurance coverage, and internal governance policies.

step1.label: Identify
step1.body: Map AI touchpoints across business units.

step2.label: Attribute
step2.body: Link literacy gaps to liability exposure zones.

step3.label: Govern
step3.item2: Mandate certification for high-risk role holders

lesson3.title: Vendor Disclosure & Governance Reporting
lesson3.intro: Executive governance requires continuous monitoring of vendor AI transparency and deployer literacy support throughout the contract lifecycle.
lesson3.paragraph1: Static due-diligence at procurement is insufficient — governance reporting must track vendor updates, model changes, and evolving regulatory expectations.
lesson3.paragraph2: This module defines the reporting cadence and documentation standards for institutional Article 4 governance committees.

step1.label: Monitor
step1.body: Track vendor model updates and disclosure changes.
step1.item2: Assess impact on operator literacy requirements

step2.label: Report
step2.body: Deliver structured governance briefings to leadership.

step3.label: Certify
step3.body: Validate executive competency via formal assessment.
step3.item1: Require Level 02 examination for governance leads

q1.prompt: A vendor claims their AI tool is 'fully automated' and requires no operator training. How should procurement respond under Article 4?
q1.context: Evaluate deployer literacy obligations regardless of vendor marketing language.

options.a: Proceed with purchase since automation reduces staffing costs
options.b: Delegate compliance verification entirely to the vendor
options.c: Reject the claim and require vendor-provided operator training materials before procurement approval
options.d: Classify the tool as low-risk based on vendor marketing materials

q2.prompt: Your legal team discovers an AI vendor updated their model without notifying your organization. Which governance process failed?
q2.context: Consider continuous vendor disclosure monitoring and contract enforcement mechanisms.

options.a: Continuous vendor disclosure monitoring and contract enforcement
options.b: Annual financial audit of vendor accounts
options.c: Employee satisfaction survey for AI tool users
options.d: Internal marketing review of vendor branding

q3.prompt: The board asks who is liable when an uncertified employee approves an AI-generated credit denial. What framework do you reference?
q3.context: Apply corporate risk attribution and literacy certification requirements.

options.d: Corporate risk attribution framework linking literacy certification to liability zones

lesson1.title: High-Risk System Classification & Audit Scope
lesson1.intro: Technical auditors must accurately classify AI deployments under EU AI Act risk tiers to scope audit procedures appropriately.
lesson1.paragraph1: Misclassification is the most common root cause of inadequate oversight architecture — auditors must validate classification against both system capability and deployment context.
lesson1.paragraph2: This module establishes the audit scope definition protocol for high-risk system validation engagements.

step1.label: Classify
step1.body: Apply EU AI Act Annex III criteria to the deployment.
step1.item1: Map system outputs to regulated use cases

step2.label: Scope
step2.body: Define audit boundaries and human oversight test points.
step2.item1: Identify critical decision nodes requiring intervention
step2.item2: Set sampling methodology for scenario validation

step3.label: Baseline
step3.item2: Archive system configuration and model provenance

lesson2.title: Human-in-the-Loop Auditing Methodology
lesson2.intro: Complex auditing workflows must validate not only that human oversight exists, but that it is exercised competently under realistic operational conditions.
lesson2.paragraph1: Auditors simulate adverse scenarios to test whether operators recognize escalation triggers and execute intervention protocols within institutional time thresholds.
lesson2.paragraph2: This module covers the Cisco-grade scenario methodology used in the formal 30-scenario certification runtime.

step1.label: Design
step1.body: Construct regulatory scenarios matching deployment risk profile.
step1.item1: Align scenarios to Article 4 oversight metrics
step1.item2: Include edge cases for fundamental rights impact

step2.label: Execute
step2.body: Observe operator responses under controlled conditions.
step2.item1: Measure intervention latency and decision quality
step2.item2: Document override rationale and escalation paths

step3.label: Score
step3.body: Apply weighted compliance scoring against institutional threshold.
step3.item1: Calculate tier-weighted percentage per scenario block

lesson3.title: Data Lineage Provenance & Ledger Hash Orchestration
lesson3.intro: Expert auditors validate that certification credentials and audit artifacts are immutably logged with cryptographic integrity on WaqfLedger.tech.
lesson3.paragraph1: Data lineage provenance tracks how training data, model versions, and operator decisions chain together for regulatory inquiry reconstruction.
lesson3.paragraph2: Ledger hash orchestration ensures that successful certifications produce verifiable AES-256 block hashes on the Sovereign Algorithmic Governance Ledger.

step1.label: Trace
step1.body: Reconstruct data lineage from source to model output.
step1.item2: Validate model version control and change logs

step2.label: Hash
step2.body: Generate integrity digest for audit artifact bundle.
step2.item2: Include examination score and tier metadata

step3.label: Register
step3.body: Commit credential hash to WaqfLedger.tech registry.
step3.item2: Provide verification URL for third-party audit

q1.prompt: An AI hiring tool is deployed internally for resume screening only, but outputs influence interview selection. How do you classify this system?
q1.context: Apply high-risk classification criteria based on deployment impact, not stated internal use labels.

options.a: High-risk AI system — employment-related decision influence regardless of internal use labeling
options.b: Minimal-risk internal productivity tool with no compliance requirements
options.c: Excluded general-purpose AI with no deployer obligations
options.d: Low-risk scheduling assistant outside Annex III scope

q2.prompt: During a HITL audit, an operator consistently overrides AI recommendations within 2 seconds without reading the output. What does this indicate?
q2.context: Evaluate whether human oversight is substantive or performative under Article 4 standards.

options.a: Exemplary efficiency demonstrating operator AI literacy mastery
options.b: Performative oversight — intervention without substantive review violates Article 4 standards
options.c: Evidence of optimal human-AI collaboration velocity
options.d: Confirmation that escalation protocols are functioning correctly

q3.prompt: A certification hash on WaqfLedger.tech fails third-party verification. What is your first investigative step?
q3.context: Consider data lineage integrity and AES-256 digest reconstruction procedures.

options.a: Issue a new certification without ledger verification
options.b: Contact the candidate to re-take the examination immediately
options.c: Reconstruct the data lineage and verify AES-256 digest integrity against source artifacts
options.d: Remove the credential from public display without investigation

q1.question: What are EU AI Act Article 4 human oversight metrics?
q1.answer: Article 4 human oversight metrics measure whether deployers and their staff possess sufficient AI literacy to operate, supervise, and intervene in AI systems responsibly — including risk recognition, escalation protocols, and transparency obligations under the EU AI Act.

q2.question: How does the 30-Scenario Diagnostic Certification Core work?
q2.answer: The diagnostic core presents 30 Cisco-grade regulatory scenarios evaluating human-in-the-loop decision-making. A weighted compliance score of 85% or higher grants an institutional certification badge with immutable logging on WaqfLedger.tech.

q3.question: Who certifies credentials on safeAI.report?
q3.answer: Certifications are issued under L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I) and the academic authority of Professor Adel El Bouzagaoui, with processing by Global Capital Intelligence LLC.

q4.answer: An upcoming Udemy micro-credential syllabus for judges, magistrates, and legal professionals covering deployer liability, high-risk AI classification, judicial human-oversight architecture, and cryptographic credential verification.

q2.answer: The standardized certification pathway established under L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I) to document deployer literacy under EU AI Act Article 4 Compliance Metrics.

shared.dataHandlingBody: Candidate assessment metrics, examination tokens, and certification state hashes are processed exclusively under EU AI Act Article 4 Compliance Metrics. All commercial settlement and payment records are managed by the verified processing entity via Stripe checkout infrastructure.
shared.dataProtectionBody: safeAI.report maintains strict conformity with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Anonymous academic telemetry arrays—aggregated scenario performance indices, compliance score distributions, and token utilization metrics—are collected solely for institutional oversight reporting. These telemetry structures are architecturally decoupled from personally identifiable information (PII): no candidate names, email addresses, government identifiers, biometric data, or free-text responses containing personal references are retained in persistent storage. Data minimization, purpose limitation, and storage limitation principles are enforced at the application layer. Where transactional contact data is submitted through institutional intake forms, such records are processed exclusively for license fulfillment correspondence and are not merged into anonymous telemetry pipelines.
shared.registryGovernanceBody: All certification events and B2B institutional license activations are cryptographically attested through verification sovereignty protocols before immutable publication to WaqfLedger.tech. Each credential issuance undergoes multi-stage validation: (1) examination integrity verification against the authorized 30-scenario Article 4 assessment matrix; (2) institutional authority confirmation under L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I); (3) AES-256 cryptographic block hashing of the certification state payload; and (4) sovereign ledger anchoring under the Global Capital Intelligence LLC registry identifiers (RNA: W343030686, SIRET: 92288647800010, ICE: 001625897000071). Immutable hashes issued to WaqfLedger.tech constitute the authoritative verification record and may not be altered, superseded, or revoked without a formally logged governance amendment event.
shared.complianceBody: Certifications are immutably logged to the Sovereign Algorithmic Governance Ledger at WaqfLedger.tech using AES-256 Cryptographic Block Hashing. Academic authority rests with L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I) under Professor Adel El Bouzagaoui.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.subtitle: Non-commercial open-access terms governing universal diagnostic access, voluntary research support grants, and platform participation under the safeAI.report ecosystem — structured entirely for public-good research, not commercial licensing.
terms.section1Body: L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I), operating through the safeAI.report platform, functions exclusively as an open-access public good instrument dedicated to supporting empirical research data compilation, doctoral oversight benchmarking, and international statutory research under EU AI Act Article 4 Compliance Metrics. The evaluation ecosystem is administered under the academic authority of Professor Adel El Bouzagaoui. Universal diagnostic access is extended at no charge to advance the standardized TOEFL for AI Literacy protocol — not as a commercial product offering or licensed software entitlement.
terms.section2Body: All certification and diagnostic evaluation tracks across the platform are complimentary and universally open to the public. Any financial participation by accredited universities or statutory bodies is structured exclusively as voluntary research support grants to subsidize sovereign node distribution, cloud bandwidth, and open-access data telemetry infrastructure — not commercial software licensing. Such contributions generate zero commercial warranties, merchant-of-record contracts, software-as-a-service licensing agreements, or service-level assurances (SLAs) of the kind associated with proprietary technology vendors. Grant participation does not confer resale rights, exclusive access tiers, or commercial entitlements of any kind.
terms.section3Body: The Cisco-grade 30-scenario evaluation matrix, scenario architecture, scoring methodologies, and certification badge frameworks constitute proprietary academic intellectual property administered under L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I) and the academic authority of Professor Adel El Bouzagaoui. Automated scraping, bulk harvesting, reverse engineering, mirror deployment, or unauthorized commercial resale of assessment content, scenario payloads, or scoring algorithms is strictly prohibited without express written authorization from the academic governing authority. Processing infrastructure is operated by Global Capital Intelligence LLC under registry identifiers disclosed in the legal anchors registry.
terms.section4Body: Compliance tracking, credential provenance indexing, and cryptographic state anchoring to WaqfLedger.tech operate on a best-effort basis for research and statutory benchmarking purposes. safeAI.report, Global Capital Intelligence LLC, and L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I) provide the evaluation runtime and verification infrastructure without warranty regarding regulatory adjudication, employment decisions, or institutional audit conclusions. Ultimate statutory accountability for AI deployment oversight, human-in-the-loop governance, and Article 4 compliance execution remains with the deploying entity — whether individual examinee, faculty cohort, or institutional registrar.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy & Registry Data Sovereignty
privacy.subtitle: Advanced data minimization and cryptographic sovereignty standards governing record integrity, permanent tamper-proof provenance, and academic telemetry under the safeAI.report ecosystem.
privacy.section1Body: The safeAI.report evaluation system is architected under a strict data minimization doctrine aligned with GDPR purpose-limitation principles. The platform deliberately avoids gathering invasive personal metrics, behavioral advertising profiles, biometric identifiers, or third-party data broker integrations. To initialize a certification validation state, the runtime requests only an authorized institutional or candidate email address and an optional display name — nothing further is required to execute the open-access diagnostic session. Response-level telemetry is processed in anonymized aggregate form before any persistence layer.
privacy.section2Body: Upon achieving a passing performance threshold (≥85%), the platform generates a 64-character SHA-256 cryptographic state hash encapsulating examination integrity markers, timestamp vectors, and registry identifiers. This hash is instantly transmitted as a non-blocking background void promise to the sovereign ledger API endpoint at waqfledger-api.vercel.app via TLS-encrypted POST to /api/v1/ledger/log-compliance, immutably anchoring the certification event to WaqfLedger.tech without interrupting the examinee experience.
privacy.section3Body: Because all certification provenance records are permanently anchored to the WaqfLedger.tech infrastructure network, they cannot be deleted, modified, or reversed once published to the sovereign ledger index. This immutability establishes an unalterable verification trail suitable for corporate audit teams, regulatory examiners, and institutional verification desks. Registry data sovereignty is maintained under L'INSTITUT ARTICLE 4 (A4I) registry identifiers (RNA: W343030686, SIRET: 92288647800010, ICE: 001625897000071). Independent verifiers may cross-reference published SHA-256 state hashes against the WaqfLedger cryptographic index without intermediary attestation from safeAI.report.
privacy.section4Body: Anonymized scoring distribution datasets derived from aggregate examination performance are retained and optimized strictly for academic reviews, longitudinal data tracking, and doctoral compilation research under EU AI Act Article 4 Compliance Metrics. These telemetry structures support scenario difficulty calibration, competency trend analysis, and sovereign cloud compute optimization — never individual profiling, marketing segmentation, or commercial data resale. All academic telemetry processing is supervised under the academic authority of Professor Adel El Bouzagaoui.

stripe.label: Stripe Payment Link Gateway Mode
stripe.description: SAFEAI_ADMIN_STRIPE_PRODUCTION: when active, institutional checkout CTAs resolve to live production Stripe Payment Links (infrastructure.stripeResearchContributorUrl / stripeNodeSponsorUrl). When inactive, checkout swaps to Stripe Test Mode sandbox links (stripeResearchContributorUrlSandbox / stripeNodeSponsorUrlSandbox) for offline validation.

ledger.label: Provenance Ledger Stream Bypass
ledger.description: Switches the public verification gateway between local database arrays and direct live WaqfLedger streams.
